items.question: A 26-year-old man with tall stature is referred to endocrinology. Which of the following steps would be most helpful in differentiating pseudoacromegaly from true acromegaly?
options: Take a family history, including questions regarding consanguinity
items.explanation: The patient's clubbing, furrowing of the forehead skin, and skinny body habitus are all characteristics of pachydermoperiostosis. These features do not typically accompany pseudoacromegaly.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: Pachydermoperiostosis is a condition that can mimic acromegaloid features but has no associated GH/IGF-1 anomalies.

items.question: Why is pachydermoperiostosis considered a pseudoacromegaly condition?
items.answer: Because it mimics acromegaloid features but has no associated GH/IGF-1 anomalies.
keyPoints: Pachydermoperiostosis can present with clubbing, furrowing of the forehead skin, and skinny body habitus
It is a condition that does not have an associated GH/IGF-1 anomaly
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: Pachydermoperiostosis is a condition that can mimic acromegaloid features but has no associated GH/IGF-1 anomalies.

items.question: How can pachydermoperiostosis be diagnosed?
items.answer: By identifying the characteristic features of clubbing, furrowing of the forehead skin, and skinny body habitus.
keyPoints: Clubbing is a key feature in diagnosing pachydermoperiostosis
Furrowing of the forehead skin can be observed as an additional clue
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: Pachydermoperiostosis is a condition that can mimic acromegaloid features but has no associated GH/IGF-1 anomalies.
